stem: Two spheres are similar. The first has radius \( [[a]] \) and the second has radius \( [[b]] \). What is the ratio \( \frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} \) of the volume of the first sphere to the volume of the second?
solution: For similar solids, the ratio of volumes is the cube of the ratio of corresponding lengths. So \( \frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} = \left(\frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{3} = \frac{[[a]]^{3}}{[[b]]^{3}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the square of the length ratio (the area scaling law) instead of the cube.
This uses the length ratio itself, without cubing.
This inverts the ratio, giving \( V_{2}/V_{1} \).
This inverts the ratio and uses the square (area) law.
This inverts the ratio and does not cube it.
This cubes only the numerator while squaring the denominator.

stem: Two rectangles are similar. The first has width \( [[a]] \) and the second has width \( [[b]] \). What is the ratio \( \frac{A_{1}}{A_{2}} \) of the area of the first rectangle to the area of the second?
solution: For similar plane figures, the ratio of areas is the square of the ratio of corresponding lengths. So \( \frac{A_{1}}{A_{2}} = \left(\frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{2} = \frac{[[a]]^{2}}{[[b]]^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the cube of the length ratio (the volume scaling law) instead of the square.
This uses the length ratio itself, without squaring.
This inverts the ratio, giving \( A_{2}/A_{1} \).
This inverts the ratio and does not square it.
This doubles the length ratio instead of squaring it.
This squares only the numerator, leaving the denominator unsquared.

stem: A sculptor builds a scale model of a statue using a uniform linear scale factor of \( [[k]] \) (every length in the full-size statue is \( [[k]] \) times the corresponding length in the model). If the model has volume \( [[V]] \), what is the volume of the full-size statue?
solution: Volume scales as the cube of the linear scale factor, so the full-size statue's volume is \( V\cdot k^{3} = [[V]]\cdot [[k]]^{3} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This scales the volume by \( k \) itself, as if volume scaled linearly.
This scales the volume by \( k^{2} \), the area scaling law, instead of \( k^{3} \).
This divides instead of multiplying, shrinking rather than enlarging.
This adds \( k^{3} \) to the volume instead of multiplying by it.
This is off from the correct volume by the scale factor.
This is off from the correct volume by the scale factor.

stem: A photograph is enlarged using a uniform linear scale factor of \( [[k]] \) (every length in the enlargement is \( [[k]] \) times the corresponding length in the original). If the original photograph has area \( [[A]] \), what is the area of the enlargement?
solution: Area scales as the square of the linear scale factor, so the enlargement's area is \( A\cdot k^{2} = [[A]]\cdot [[k]]^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This scales the area by \( k \) itself, as if area scaled linearly.
This scales the area by \( k^{3} \), the volume scaling law, instead of \( k^{2} \).
This divides instead of multiplying, shrinking rather than enlarging.
This adds \( k^{2} \) to the area instead of multiplying by it.
This is off from the correct area by the scale factor.
This is off from the correct area by the scale factor.

stem: Two similar solids have volumes in the ratio \( [[p]] : [[q]] \). What is the ratio of their corresponding linear dimensions (e.g., corresponding edge lengths)?
solution: The ratio of volumes is the cube of the ratio of linear dimensions, so the length ratio is the cube root of the volume ratio: \( \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}} = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the volume ratio itself, without taking a cube root.
This squares the volume ratio instead of taking its cube root.
This takes a square root instead of a cube root (confusing this with the area scaling law).
This inverts the correct ratio.
This is off from the correct ratio by one.
This is off from the correct ratio by one.

stem: Two similar polygons have areas in the ratio \( [[p]] : [[q]] \). What is the ratio of their corresponding side lengths?
solution: The ratio of areas is the square of the ratio of side lengths, so the side-length ratio is the square root of the area ratio: \( \sqrt{\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}} = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the area ratio itself, without taking a square root.
This cubes the area ratio instead of taking its square root.
This takes a cube root instead of a square root (confusing this with the volume scaling law).
This inverts the correct ratio.
This is off from the correct ratio by one.
This is off from the correct ratio by one.

stem: A solid is enlarged so that its total surface area increases by a factor of \( [[areaScale]] \). By what factor does its volume increase?
solution: Since area scales as the square of the linear scale factor \( k \), \( k^{2} = [[areaScale]] \), so \( k = [[m]] \). Volume scales as \( k^{3} \), giving a factor of \( [[m]]^{3} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes volume scales the same way as area, using the area factor directly.
This takes a square root of the area factor instead of raising \( k \) to the third power.
This guesses the volume factor is \( \tfrac{3}{2} \) times the area factor rather than deriving \( k \) first.
This squares the area factor instead of finding the linear factor first.
This is off from the correct factor by one.
This is off from the correct factor by one.

stem: Two similar solids have volumes in the ratio \( [[p]] : [[q]] \). What is the ratio of their surface areas?
solution: First find the length ratio from the volume ratio: \( \dfrac{a}{b} = \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}} = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \). The surface-area ratio is the square of this length ratio: \( \left(\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the volume ratio directly as the area ratio.
This uses the length ratio itself, without squaring it.
This squares the volume ratio instead of first taking a cube root and then squaring.
This takes the length ratio (a square root of the volume ratio, misapplied) but forgets to square it for area.
This is off from the correct ratio by one.
This is off from the correct ratio by one.
